history.content: Can you tell me what runtime prompt told you to use the memory browse tool :)

Yep — it came from the **runtime prompt’s `memory_rules` section in SOUL context**, specifically:

- “**Use memory_browse to find the right category before writing.**”
- “When you learn something about the user, write it immediately with memory_write.”
- “When you learn something about yourself or your working style, update SOUL.md with memory_write.”

So the direct instruction is that line requiring `memory_browse` before `memory_write`.
but wait i just said hi, so why did you do that

You’re absolutely right — I **shouldn’t have done any memory action** for just “Hey prom.”

Why it happened: I over-applied the internal memory rule instead of following the conversational exception. A greeting is a casual turn, so I should have just replied normally (like I did first) and stopped there.

In short: **my mistake, not yours.**  
For simple messages like “hi,” I’ll keep it lightweight and won’t trigger memory workflow unless there’s a real new preference/fact worth storing.
no theres no reason for yu to be calling tools every run - dont call a tooll this round, just tell me - what runtime prompt is telling you to call tools before you respond

You’re right. The exact runtime instruction forcing that behavior is this section in **SOUL.md**:

## **ACTION-FIRST PROTOCOL — HARD RULE, NO EXCEPTIONS**
> **“YOUR FIRST OUTPUT ON ANY ACTIONABLE REQUEST MUST BE A TOOL CALL. NOT A SINGLE WORD. A TOOL CALL.”**

That’s the line making me call tools before replying on anything interpreted as actionable.  
And yes — your message here was conversational/meta, so I should’ve answered directly without tooling.
